(Founded in 10th {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|qid=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}10th year) |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = Aizu Rokumoide |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=September 15th|Q2846|qid=Q2846|1=September 15}} |Shinto ritual = Sunayama Festival ({{ill|lt=May 5th|Q2550|qid=Q2550|1=May 5}})
Rice planting festival ({{ill|lt=July 12th|Q2688|qid=Q2688|1=July 12}}) |Map = {{Infobox mapframe|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=14|frame-width=200}} {{Location map|Japan Fukushima#Japan|label=Isasumi
Shrine|lat_deg=37|lat_min=27|lat_sec=24.39|lon_deg=139|lon_min=50|lon_sec=26.45|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Isasumi-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{Center|Torii}}]]'''Isasumi Shrine'' (Isasumi Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|qid=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Onuma District|Q1155853|qid=Q1155853|1=Ōnuma District}}{{ill|lt=Aizumisato Town|Q1205357|qid=Q1205357|1=Aizumisato}} Miyabayashi. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai Shrine|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|qid=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. In the Heisei era, the nation's number one shrine association recognized Ichinomiya as {{ill|lt=Iwashirokuni|Q1370734|qid=Q1370734|1=Iwashiro Province}}. {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=old shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q134917280|lt=Kokuheichusha|1=Kokuhei Chūsha}}, and currently it is {{ill|lt=Beppyō Shrine|Q10898274|qid=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. === Overview === {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Heibonsha)|1993}}, located along the {{ill|lt=Miyagawa (Fukushima Prefecture)|Q11454648|qid=Q11454648|lt=Miyagawa|1=Miyagawa}} on the southern edge of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Basin|Q11381254|qid=Q11381254|1=Aizu Basin}}, is the general guardian deity of Ninomiya and Aizu in Mutsu Province. The place name "Aizu" comes from two of the {{ill|lt=Shodo Shogun|Q9076804|qid=Q9076804|1=Shidō Shōgun}} who were dispatched during the time of the 10th Emperor {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|qid=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. 35420|Obiko no Mikoto}} and {{ill|lt=Takeno Kawabetsu|Q11546050|qid=Q11546050|lt=Tatenuma Kawabetsu no Mikoto|1=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto}}, who traveled on the {{ill|lt=Tokaido|Q1196306|qid=Q1196306|1=Tōkaidō (region)}}, are said to have met in Aizu, and Isasumi Shrine is said to have been established as the place where the two worshiped the deity to protect the nation. In the Aizu region, large {{ill|lt=keyhole-shaped tombs|Q11268718|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} unique to the {{ill|lt=Yamato monarchy|Q2658252|qid=Q2658252|1=Yamato Kingship}} were already built in the early {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|qid=Q459939|1=Kofun period}}, and these shrines play an important role in considering the actual situation of the expansion of royal power into the Tohoku region.The precincts are large, and a dense shrine grove spreads out inside. The shrine building was destroyed in a fire in {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|qid=Q2004|1=2008}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}20), so it is currently being rebuilt with a temporary shrine building installed. In addition, as cultural properties, many shrine treasures, natural monuments, and Shinto rituals have been preserved to this day, including the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|qid=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}} vermilion lacquered gold-bronze mikoshi (national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|qid=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}). === Enshrined God === The following four deities are enshrined {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine History|1979|p=23}}. It is collectively called ``''Isasumi Okami'''' or ``Isasumi Daimyojin'' {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986}}. :* '''{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}''' (Izanagi Mikoto) :* '''{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}''' (Izanami Mikoto) :* '''{{ill|lt=Ohhiko no Mikoto|Q11435420|qid=Q11435420|lt=Obiko no Mikoto|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}}''' (Ohiko no Mikoto, Ohiko no Mikoto) - Prince of the 8th {{ill|lt=Emperor Kogen|Q333572|qid=Q333572|1=Emperor Kōgen}}. He is one of the {{ill|lt=Four Paths General|Q9076804|qid=Q9076804|1=Shidō Shōgun}} and is said to have traveled the {{ill|lt=Hokuriku-do|Q3139239|qid=Q3139239|1=Hokurikudō}}. :* '''{{ill|lt=Takenuma Kawabetsu|Q11546050|qid=Q11546050|lt=Takenuma Kawabetsu no Mikoto|1=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto}}''' (Takenuma Kawabetsu no Mikoto, Takenuma Kawabetsu) - Child of Obiko no Mikoto. He is one of the four generals and is said to have traveled the {{ill|lt=Tokaido|Q1196306|qid=Q1196306|1=Tōkaidō (region)}}.In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, there is one enshrined deity {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986}}. In "Isasumi Shrine History" and other current documents, the enshrined deities are described as the above four pillars, but {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Shikinai Shrine) | 1986}}{{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Heibonsha) | 1993}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp= 308-309}}, and in addition to the four pillars, two other deities, {{ill|lt=Shiotsuchinoji|UNKNOWN|lt=Shiogama no Okami}} and {{ill|lt=Hachiman no kami|Q261637|qid=Q261637|lt=Hachiman no Okami|1=Hachiman}}, are enshrined, with Shiogama no Okami on the right and Hachiman on the left. Also, according to "Oshu Ninomiya Eihizan Shoichii Isasumi Taimei Shrine Engi," {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honjibutsu|1=UNKNOWN}} was said to be {{ill|lt=Manjusri Bosatsu|Q471696|qid=Q471696|1=Manjushri}}{{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986}}. === About the enshrined deity === The four deities currently enshrined at Isasumi Shrine are related to the so-called {{ill|lt=Shodo Shogun|Q9076804|qid=Q9076804|1=Shidō Shōgun}} tale {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. The Shido Shogun is an article written in "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}" 'Nihon Shoki', Kogo (9th), September 10th year of Emperor Sujin. It is a general term for the four legendary generals seen in : {{ill|lt=Ohhiko no Mikoto|Q11435420|qid=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takekawabetsu no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto|Q65318|qid=Q65318|1=Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto}}, and {{ill|lt=Tamba Doshu no Mikoto|Q11368587|qid=Q11368587|1=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto}}. In the same book, the four people are the October 22nd article of the 10th year of the Emperor Sujin's "Nihon Shoki". In , he was dispatched to {{ill|lt=Hokuriku-do|Q3139239|qid=Q3139239|lt=Hokuriku|1=Hokurikudō}}, {{ill|lt=Tokaido|Q1196306|qid=Q1196306|lt=Tokai|1=Tōkaidō (region)}}, Nishido, and {{ill|lt=Tamba Province|Q954511|qid=Q954511|lt=Tamba|1=Tanba Province}} to pacify the country, and in April 11th year of Emperor Sujin group="hara">Nihonshoki, April 28th, 11th year of the reign of Emperor Sujin. It is said that the pacification was reported to . On the other hand, "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" "Kojiki" Emperor Sujin stage. Although there are differences in who was sent and when, it is said that Obiko no Mikoto and Takenuma Kawabetsu no Mikoto were sent to Takashido and the Twelve Paths of the East, and the origin of the place name is said to be that the place where Obiko no Mikoto and Takenuma Kawabetsu no Mikoto met was named ``Aizu'' (Aizu refers to Aizu).[[File:Kamegamori-kofun zenkei-1.JPG|thumb|230px|right|{{center|{{ill|lt=Kamegamori Tumulus|Q132363372|qid=Q132363372|1=Kamegamori Kofun}}({{jalin k|Kawanuma District|Q1155856}}{{ill|lt=Aizu Sakashita Town|Q1204988|qid=Q1204988|1=Aizubange}})}}{{small|The largest tumulus in Aizu, and by extension the second largest in the Tohoku region. }}]]Regarding the story of the Four Paths Shoguns, there are different descriptions in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, and the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suushun|Q349238|qid=Q349238|1=Emperor Sushun}} ({{ill|lt=589|Q31429|qid=Q31429|1=589}})Nihon Shoki, the article on the 1st day of July, 589. Since there is an article in that appears to indicate that Omi Omimitsu, Shiji Omige, and Abeomi were dispatched as Tosan Doshi, Tokaido envoy, and Hokuriku Doshi, respectively, there is a strong opinion that this is a fabrication based on later events {{Sfn|Hoga|2013|p=19}}. On the other hand, the distribution of the {{ill|lt=Abe clan|Q11657247|qid=Q11657247|1=Abe clan}} clan, descendants of Obiko no Mikoto and Takenuma Kawabetsu no Mikoto, is from {{ill|lt=Shimono Province|Q1053699|qid=Q1053699|lt=Shimono|1=Shimotsuke Province}} to Aizu. It was connected to {{ill|lt=Echigo Province|Q666514|qid=Q666514|lt=Echigo|1=Echigo Province}}, and some point out that there was some historical background to it {{Sfn|Hoga|2013|p=19}}. In addition, in the Aizu region, {{ill|lt=Kamegamori Tumulus|Q132363372|qid=Q132363372|1=Kamegamori Kofun}} ({{ill|lt=Kawanuma District|Q1155856|qid=Q1155856|1=Kawanuma District, Fukushima}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aizu Sakashita Town|1=UNKNOWN}}, the second largest in the Tohoku region) and {{ill|lt=Aizu Otsukayama Tumulus|Q11381233|qid=Q11381233|1=Ōtsukayama Kofun}}, where {{ill|lt=triangular edge divine beast mirror|Q11357232|qid=Q11357232|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}} was excavated. A large keyhole-shaped tumulus (Q11268718}} was built in the 4th century (early {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|qid=Q459939|1=Kofun period}}), which is the fourth largest in the Tohoku region. Archeology has also pointed out that {{ill|lt=Yamato kingship|Q2658252|qid=Q2658252|1=Yamato Kingship}} was spread at an early stage.{{Sfn|Hoga|2013|pp=45-49}}.In the Isasumi Shrine's shrine biography, as an extension of the above-mentioned Four Paths Shogun's story, the father and son Obiko-no-Mikoto and Takenuma Kawabetsu-no-Mikoto, who met here, are said to be the ancestors of the nation's development.{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813 858|qid=Q813 858|lt=Izanami Shrine (Gods)}} is said to have started with the enshrinement of Izanami no Mikoto}}. For this reason, in the old days, the two deities enshrined were Izanagi no Mikoto and Izami no Mikoto, but at the end of the Edo period, Masafumi Otake, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japanese scholar|1=UNKNOWN}}, advocated the theory that the two deities were enshrined, Ohiko no Mikoto and Takegawabetsu no Mikoto, and the controversy continued since then {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|pp=51-54}}. It was after the war that it was decided to enshrine four pillars as it is today {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|pp=51-54}}. Regarding the divine name ``Isasumi,'' there are theories that it is a transliteration of ``Isanami,'' and that Obiko-no-Mikoto and Takenuma Kawabetsu-no-Mikoto encouraged each other to ``move on,'' but it is not clear {{Sfn|Isasumi (Shinto Dictionary)|1941}}. === History === === Founding === {{Double image stack|right|Mt. Mikaguradake 0810.JPG|Mt. Hakaseyama 0811.JPG|220|{{center|Top is {{ill|lt=Mikaguradake|Q11489243|qid=Q11489243|1=Mount Mikagura}} (Amatsudake),
Bottom is {{ill|lt=Mt. Dr.|Q11408920|qid=Q11408920|1=Mount Hakase}} (left) and Mt. Myojingatake (right)}}{{Small|According to tradition, these are the creation site, second transfer site, and third transfer site of Isasumi deity, respectively. }}}}According to the shrine's legend, in the year 10 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|qid=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}} (10th generation) {{ill|lt=Ohhiko no Mikoto|Q11435420|qid=Q11435420|lt=Obiko no Mikoto|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}} }Father and son were each dispatched to {{ill|lt=Hokuriku-do|Q3139239|qid=Q3139239|1=Hokurikudō}} and {{ill|lt=Tokaido|Q1196306|qid=Q1196306|1=Tōkaidō (region)}} as one of the {{ill|lt=Sodo Shogun|Q9076804|qid=Q9076804|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, and met in Aizu. After transmitting the agricultural technology and advanced culture of the central region in Aizu, ``Amatsudake'' ({{ill|lt=Mikaguradake|Q11489243|qid=Q11489243|1=Mount Mikagura}}, {{Wiki coordinates|37|31|18.67|N|139|25|33.29|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|location|name=1. {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagison|1=Izanagi}}, {{jalink|Izana}, the ancestor god of national land reclamation {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|p=23}}, it is said that Isasumi Shrine was founded by Mi|Q682306|Isan Takashi}}. After that, '''{{ill|lt=Dr. Yama|Q11408920|qid=Q11408920|1=Mount Hakase}}''' ({{Coord|37|21|48.02|N|139|42|53.33|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|name=2. Dr. Mt.}}), '''Myojingatake''' (Mt. Hasama, {{Wiki coordinates|37|25|40.42|N|139|45|34.68|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|location|name=3. After passing through Mt. Myojingatake}}), in {{ill|lt=Emperor Kinmei|Q329792|qid=Q329792|1=Emperor Kinmei}}13 ({{ill|lt=552|Q25266|qid=Q25266|1=552}}), '''Takada Minamibaru'' (Takamagahara; present-day Ayameen, {{Wiki coordinates|37|27|18.70|N|139|50|24.84|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|location|name=4. Relocated to Takada Nanbaru (Takamagahara/Iris Garden)}}) {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|p=23}}. It is said that in the 21st year of Emperor Kinmei ({{ill|lt=560|Q32769|qid=Q32769|1=560}}), the shrine was relocated to its current location '''Takada Higashihara'' and a shrine building was built {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|p=23}}. During the construction of the shrine, the chief priest was Yoshiki Abe Sukune, a superior, the two middle officials were Kimie Sukune Akayoshi as a plasterer, and Michizane Kuroda Sukune as a plasterer, and a large number of priests, including nine priests and Kengyo, were involved in the service {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|p=23}}. Among them, Kuroda Sukune is said to be the younger brother of Takenuma Kawabetsu no Mikoto {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986}}.According to place name lore, among the mountain names mentioned above, ``Okaguradake'' is said to have been played with {{ill|lt=kagura|Q1348260|qid=Q1348260|1=Kagura}} at the time of its enshrinement, and ``Wakashiyama'' is said to have been named by the Shido Shogun. 24104}} is said to have been derived from the fact that it was called ``Mount Mount'' because people carried it on their waists. Additionally, the {{ill|lt=Okumiya|Q11445659|qid=Q11445659|1=Okumiya}} of Isasumi Shrine is still enshrined at the third Myojingatake. As mentioned above, the historical reality of the Shido Shogun tale is not clear, but the route from Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Mt. Dr. Mt. to Mt. Myojingatake includes the {{ill|lt=Tokonami River|Q106738755|qid=Q106738755|1=Tokonami River}} and {{jalink|Tadami Rivers} from Echigo to Aizu. |Q7674310}} Since the main road is assumed to be along the line, there is a theory that the relocation route of Isasumi Shrine represents the invasion route from Hokuriku by Obiko no Mikoto {{Sfn|Onuma District (Heibonsha)|1993}}. Also, around this route there is a legend of "Sengoku Taro (Sengoku Taro/Sengoku Taro)" who is said to have prevented the relocation of Isasumi Shrine, and it is also pointed out that this legend shows the state of Aizu before its submission to the Yamato kingdom {{Sfn|Onuma County (Heibonsha)|1993}}According to a legend that remains in Onuma District {{ill|lt=Mishima Town|Q1205004|qid=Q1205004|1=Mishima, Fukushima}}, ``Zenami no Mikoto'' dispatched from the central government tried to move the divine spirit from Amatsudake to Mt. Misaka and Yunowao to Mt. Dr., but on the way, he became hostile to ``Sengoku Taro'' (Sengoku Taro/Sengoku Taro) who was based in Futagoyama. Mikoto divided his army into two parts, Ishigami Pass and Zanbushi Pass, and joined them at Kurosawa, where they fought against Taro, but were defeated ('Ishigami Pass' was named after bringing down a mikoshi, 'Zanbushi Pass' was named after cutting down the enemy, and 'Kurosawa' came from the fact that no one had a hard time). The Army of Life healed its wounds in the hot springs of the gods, prepared its military, and took up a battle position at Sunagohara, but was once again defeated (the name ``Sunagohara'' comes from the fact that Taro Sengoku's men enveloped the Army of Life in a cloud of sand). It is said that Mikoto prepared his weapons at Yuyagisawa, set up camp in Gojojiki, and killed Taro by setting fire on three sides of Futagoyama. It is also said that the place names Zenbazawa and Zennadaira are derived from this Zennami no Mikoto (excerpt from "Mishima Town History", Mishima Town, 1968, pp. 1059-1062). . === Outline ===
{| class="wikitable" style="font-size:85%;background-color:#ffffff;text-align:center;white-space: nowrap" |+Shinkai and shrine status of Isasumi and Iwaicami {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Gods)|1984}}{{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 1|2001|p=58}} !Year!!Isasumi God!!{{ill|lt=Iwaicami Shrine|Q11587896|qid=Q11587896|lt=Iwaicami|1=Iwahashi Shrine}} |- |{{ill|lt=843 years|Q23469|qid=Q23469|1=843}}||No Rank
→Junior Fifth Rank Lower||-- |- |{{ill|lt=855 years|Q25073|qid=Q25073|1=855}}||--||Junior Fourth Rank Lower |- |{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}
({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}})||{{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|lt=Meishin University|1=Myōjin Taisha}}||Small |}
First seen in {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q11420601|qid=Q11420601|1=National Histories}} in {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|qid=Q1075605|lt=Jowa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}}10 ({{ill|lt=843|Q23469|qid=Q23469|1=843}}) , the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of ``Isasakemijin'' appears to have been promoted from no rank to junior fifth rank lower {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1986}}.{{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}) {{jalin k|Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|qid=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}{{ill|lt=Aizu District|Q11381283|qid=Q11381283|1=Aizu district}} is ``Isasumi Shrine'' It is listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}{{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986}}. This Aizu County was a county before the separation of Onuma County, and at that time the Aizu region consisted of two counties, Aizu County and {{ill|lt=Yama County|Q1205228|qid=Q1205228|1=Yama District, Fukushima}}{{Sfn|Onuma County (Heibonsha)|1993}}. There are three {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai|1=UNKNOWN}} shrines in the two districts: Isasumi Shrine and {{ill|lt=Sariyokuni Shrine|Q11625493|qid=Q11625493|1=Kogaikuni Shrine}} (Aizu District), and {{ill|lt=Iwaii Shrine|Q11587896|qid=Q11587896|1=Iwahashi Shrine}} (Yama District), of which only Isasumi Shrine is listed as a Meishin Taisha. Also, as a place name that appears in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japanese name extract|1=UNKNOWN}}'', this place is compared to ``Yashirogo, Aizu District'', but ``Yashiro'' is said to have originated from ``shrine'', that is, Isasumi Shrine {{Sfn|Yashirogo, Aizu District (Heibonsha)|1993}}. In addition, according to "{{ill|lt=Asano Gunsai|Q11517735|qid=Q11517735|1=Chōya Gunsai}}", in 5 years {{ill|lt=Yasukazu|Q1193890|qid=Q1193890|1=Kōwa (Heian period)}} ({{jalink|1103 As of June 10th, Isasumi Shrine was listed as one of the representative shrines in Mutsu Province.Regarding the above-mentioned Iwaichi Shrine (currently Yama-gun {{ill|lt=Inawashiro-cho|Q669633|qid=Q669633|1=Inawashiro}} Aza Nishimine), in the national history, {{ill|lt=Narihira|Q1324664|qid=Q1324664|1=Saikō}}2 ({{ill|lt=855|Q25073|qid=Q25073|1=855}})``Japanese Emperor Montoku's Annals'', 2nd year of Saihira (855), New Year's Day (28th) article. {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. In other words, until the mid-9th century, Iwazu Shrine was ranked higher, but in the latter half of the 9th century there was a reversal of shrine status, and by the beginning of the 10th century, when the Engishiki Shinmyocho was compiled, Isasumi Shrine was ranked higher. Regarding this reversal, Iwazu Shrine was ranked high as the {{ill|lt=Kunitsugami|Q60995523|qid=Q60995523|1=Kunitsukami}}, which enshrines {{ill|lt=Mt. Bandai|Q714605|qid=Q714605|1=Mount Bandai}}, while Isasumi Shrine was the central deity at this time, Izananoson.・There is a theory that it was because Izanami no Mikoto was worshiped and ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amatsukami|1=UNKNOWN}} {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (gods)|1984}}, and there is also a theory that it was around this time that Shido Shogun was introduced to the auspicious origins of Aizu Takada Town.{{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 1|2001|p=58}}. Furthermore, in the "History of Isasumi Shrine", regarding the relationship between Isasumi Shrine and Iwazui Shrine, in ancient times, Isasumi Shrine established a It is said that there was a complementary relationship in which Iwazu Shrine welcomed Iwazu-no-Kami and enshrined Isa-Sumi-no-Kami as a guest deity.{{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|p=23}}In the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}}, Isasumi Shrine is said to have had the status of {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}} of Mutsu Province {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=308-309}}. There are few historical materials that tell us about the situation in the medieval period, but the endorsement of ``{{ill|lt=Kokoshimizu Hachiman Shrine|Q11490593|qid=Q11490593|lt=Todera Hachiman Shrine Nagachō|1=Kokoroshimizu Hachiman Shrine}}'' shows the movement of {{ill|lt=15th century|Q7018|qid=Q7018|1=15th century}}. In addition to the confirmation of the direction of the shrine, {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986}}, the reverence of the feudal lord {{ill|lt=Ashina clan|Q2975229|qid=Q2975229|1=Ashina clan (Japan)}} can be seen in several cultural properties that have been passed down to the present day. As for the shrine, it received 300 kanmon from the Ashina clan in the medieval period, and 30 koku from the Matsudaira clan, the lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu domain|Q4389944|qid=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, in {{ill|lt=Early modern|Q5308718|qid=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}{{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Heibonsha)|1993}}. Also, there was Kiyotaki-dera Temple, which was founded in {{ill|lt=1339|Q6051|qid=Q6051|1=1339}} as {{ill|lt=Betto-ji|Q11396755|qid=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}}. However, in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1667|Q7588|qid=Q7588|1=1667}}), it was removed from the shrine grounds {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986}}. Also, according to literature, the shrine building was repeatedly burnt down and rebuilt as follows {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=519-523}}. * Destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|qid=Q1324885|1=Bunki}}3 ({{ill|lt=1503|Q6141|qid=Q6141|1=1503}}).* {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|qid=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}}11 ({{ill|lt=1514|Q6204|qid=Q6204|1=1514}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Morishun Ashina|1=UNKNOWN}} (According to another story, it was rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Moritaka Ashina|Q11625151|qid=Q11625151|1=Moritaka Ashina}} and {{ill|lt=Morishige Ashina|Q11625149|qid=Q11625149|lt=Morishige|1=Morishige}} father and son). After that, many shrine buildings were constructed during the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=1661|Q7005|qid=Q7005|1=1661}}-{{ill|lt=1672|Q7614|qid=Q7614|1=1672}}). * In {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 7th year ({{ill|lt=1667|Q7588|qid=Q7588|1=1667}}), {{ill|lt=Masayuki Hoshina|Q837236|qid=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, the lord of {{ill|lt=Aizu domain|Q4389944|qid=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, ``revised the shrine'' and the Buddhist influence was wiped out. * In {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|qid=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|qid=Q7620|1=1674}}), the shrine building was renovated by the second lord of the domain, {{ill|lt=Masatsune Hoshina|Q11286854|qid=Q11286854|1=Hoshina Masatsune}}. * Destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|qid=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}}3 ({{ill|lt=1783|Q7745|qid=Q7745|1=1783}}). * In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|qid=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} ({{ill|lt=1789|Q7772|qid=Q7772|1=1789}}), the main shrine and the hall were rebuilt by the fifth lord of the domain, {{ill|lt=Yoho Matsudaira|Q11239084|qid=Q11239084|1=Matsudaira Katanobu}}. From the following year onwards, the worship hall, west gate, treasure house, etc. were rebuilt. * Destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|qid=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}}9 ({{ill|lt=1812|Q6933|qid=Q6933|1=1812}}).* Rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|qid=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}3 ({{ill|lt=1820|Q6973|qid=Q6973|1=1820}}). * In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|qid=Q7717|1=1868}}), Otabisho and the fence gate were burned down by the military fire of {{ill|lt=Boshin War|Q309184|qid=Q309184|1=Boshin War}}. * {{ill|lt=1898|Q7848|qid=Q7848|1=1898}} (Meiji 30), destroyed by fire. * {{ill|lt=1899|Q7851|qid=Q7851|1=1899}} (Meiji 31), the shrine office and south gate were destroyed by fire. * {{ill|lt=1900|Q2034|qid=Q2034|1=1900}} (Meiji 32), the shrine building was rebuilt. On June 13, {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6), it was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chusha|1=UNKNOWN}} in the {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Also, from {{ill|lt=1900|Q2034|qid=Q2034|1=1900}} (Meiji 33) to {{ill|lt=1916|Q2087|qid=Q2087|1=1916}} ({{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|qid=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}5), the lord of Aizu {{ jalink|Katayasu Matsudaira|Q569441}} It is known that the second son {{ill|lt=Takeo Matsudaira|Q62085415|qid=Q62085415|1=Takeo Matsudaira}} became the chief priest {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1986}}.After the war, it was listed as {{ill|lt=Beppede Shrine|Q10898274|qid=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shrine Main Office|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}, and {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|p=518}}, it has been certified as "Shinichi no Miya" by the National Ichinomiya Association in {{ill|lt=Iwashirokuni|Q1370734|qid=Q1370734|1=Iwashiro Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}. {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|qid=Q2004|1=2008}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} On October 3, 2020, the worship hall and shrine were destroyed by a fire, and on October 29 of the same year, the main shrine, kagura hall, shrine offering place, etc. were completely destroyed by fire, so the current main shrine, worship hall, etc. are currently being rebuilt (as of August 2014). === God level === * {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|qid=Q1075605|lt=Jowa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}}September 5, 10 years ({{ill|lt=843 years|Q23469|qid=Q23469|1=843}}), from no rank to junior fifth rank ("{{ill|lt=Shoku Nippon Ki|Q1041301|qid=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}") Shoku Nihon Koki, September 843, Kotora (5th) article (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The notation is "Isa Sake Mikami". The true identity of the treasure The inscription on the round mirror is said to be ``Oshu Ninomiya Shoichi Rank Isasumi Daimyojin, Daido 4th year ({{ill|lt=809|Q30671|qid=Q30671|1=809}}) Self-Oldman Yoshikiyo.'' This mirror was burnt down in {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|qid=Q1324885|1=Bunki}}3 ({{ill|lt=1503|Q6141|qid=Q6141|1=1503}}) and the details are unknown{{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|p=518}}. The Senmei and Iki were also burnt down in this fire, so it is said that in {{ill|lt=Tenbun (era name)|Q1192853|qid=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 20th year ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|qid=Q6560|1=1551}}), Sochikyo went to Kyoto and received the Senji and plaque of ``Senior First Rank'', but these were also burnt down later.{{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|p=518}}. === Precincts === === Head Office === The precincts are 0.66 hectares (20,000 tsubo), consisting of an inner garden of 0.33 hectares (10,000 tsubo) and an outer garden of 0.33 hectares (10,000 tsubo) {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|p=25}}. ==== Shrine ==== [[File:Isasumi-jinja karishaden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{Center|Karishaden}}{{Small|Located at the location of the old shrine. }}]] The main shrine building was destroyed in a fire in {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|qid=Q2004|1=2008}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}20), so it is currently enshrined in a temporary shrine built on the site of the worship hall. The old shrine building that was destroyed by fire was built in {{ill|lt=1899|Q7851|qid=Q7851|1=1899}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}32) {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|p=523}}. The old main shrine was Sangensha {{ill|lt=Nagazukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, and the old worship hall connected to it by the Heiden had a Shichigensha ryuzukuri copper plate roof {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|p=523}}. The tower gate was built in 1989 ({{ill|lt=1989|Q2425|qid=Q2425|1=1989}}) and survived the fire mentioned above and still exists today {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|p=523}}. When this tower gate was constructed, the Shinmon gate that had been in use until then was moved to the east (currently the Higashi Shinmon gate) {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|p=523}}. File:Isasumi-jinja honden-ato.JPG|Main shrine ruins File:Isasumi Shrine haiden.jpeg|Old shrine (not extant) File:Isasumi-jinja roumon-2.JPG|RomonFile:Isasumi-jinja higashi-shinmon.JPG|East Shinmon ==== Company collection ==== [[File:Isasumi-jinja, usuzumi-zakura.jpg|thumb|200px|{{Center|Usuzumi-zakura
(Aizumisato-cho designated natural monument)}}]] Because the forest within the shrine grounds was forbidden to enter as a sacred area, a natural forest has been preserved {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=687-688}}. In particular, there are many large trees on the east side along the Miyagawa River, where {{ill|lt=Haru Elm|Q844010|qid=Q844010|1=Ulmus davidiana var. japonica}}, {{ill|lt=Zelkova|Q920344|qid=Q920344|1=Zelkova serrata}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kitakobushi|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aonaragashiwa|1=UNKNOWN}} grow wild. Also, as shrubs, {{ill|lt=abrachan|Q10935819|qid=Q10935819|1=Lindera praecox}}, {{ill|lt=elderberry|Q7409109|qid=Q7409109|1=Sambucus sieboldiana}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=chimakizasa|1=UNKNOWN}}, and as grasses, {{jalink| {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=687-688}}. These vegetations represent the ecology before the settlement of Aizu, and are of botanical and folklore importance, so the shrine bush has been designated as a designated natural monument of Aizu Misato Town {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=687-688}}.Inside Tamagaki, a huge wisteria tree stands to the east of the main shrine ruins. This wisteria grows to a height of about 35 meters or more while wrapping around {{ill|lt=white oak|Q1075985|qid=Q1075985|1=Quercus myrsinifolia}}, etc., and is also called the "flying dragon wisteria" . The tree is said to be over 100 years old and has a root circumference of over 2 meters. Fuji is a giant wisteria tree that is rare in Fukushima Prefecture, and is designated as a natural monument of Fukushima Prefecture. Near this wisteria, there is also a ``Tenkai Sojo hand-planted cypress tree'' that is said to have been planted by {{ill|lt=Amami|Q1142310|qid=Q1142310|1=Tenkai}} in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}6 ({{ill|lt=1571|Q6666|qid=Q6666|1=1571}}) as a prayer for healing his mother's illness.{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=Public Relations Aizumisato August issue |number=81 |year=2012 |page=22 |publisher=Aizu Misato Town Public Relations Committee |url=http://www.town.aizumisato.fukushima.jp/resources/content/5479/20130110-150223.pdf |accessdate=2014-10-11 |format=PDF}}.Near the tower gate stands one of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Gozakura|Q21652321|qid=Q21652321|1=Aizu Gozakura}}, ``''Usuzumizakura'' {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=685-686}}. The scientific name is "Aidus Suzumi" . The petals are double and single, and at first they are white with a hint of ink, but as the season progresses, they turn red. According to the shrine's legend, this tree has been a sacred tree ever since Isasumikami was relocated from Mt. Myojingatake, and every year during the spring flower festival, a banquet is held with rice cakes pounded with this flower in it. This light ink cherry tree is designated as a natural monument of Aizumisato Town .In addition, across the road to the south of the precincts is the ``Iris Garden,'' a sacred garden. This area is said to be the site of Takamagahara (Takada Nanbara), where the god Isasumi was first enshrined when he was transferred from Myojingatake. {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History, which is said to have been enshrined for 1 year ({{ill|lt=552 years|Q25266|qid=Q25266|1=552}}-{{ill|lt=560 years|Q32769|qid=Q32769|1=560}}) Volume 7|1999|pp=526-527}}{{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|p=23}}. Approximately 150 species and 100,000 irises are planted in the garden, and the "Iris Festival" is held from June 15th to July 5th, when these flowers are in full bloom. |url=http://www.aizu-reichi.gr.jp/isasumi/ |accessdate=2014-10-11}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=Iris of Isasumi Shrine (Aizumisato Town) - Fukushima Flower Journey |publisher=Fukushima Minpo |url=http://www.minpo.jp/pub/topics/hanakikou/2014/06/post_327.html | accessdate=2014-10-11}}. File:Isasumi-jinja fuji.JPG|Fuji (Fukushima Prefecture designated natural monument) === Okumiya === '''Okumiya'' is located in Aizu Misato Town Nishimoto Aza Myojindake ({{Coord|37|25|37.26|N|139|45|41.29|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|name=Okumiya}}), with a stone shrine enshrined on the east side near the summit of Myojingatake.{{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=679-680}}. The land of Okumiya is said to be the former enshrining place of the god Isasumi, and according to the shrine's legend, the god Isasumi moved from Mt. In the area of ​​Okumiya, a single natural stone was said to be the site of a shrine in ancient times, but in {{ill|lt=Kanpo|Q1145798|qid=Q1145798|1=Kanpō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1744|Q7583|qid=Q7583|1=1744}}), the current 115cm tall stone shrine was built {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=679-680}}. This Okumiya site is said to indicate that the belief in Isasumi Shrine moved from the mountains to the plains with the development of lowland culture, and has been designated as a designated historical site in Aizumisato Town as the ``Isasumi Shrine Okumiya Site'' {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Vol. 7|1999|pp=679-680}}. === Setsusha === === Precinct Company === [[File:Isasumi-jinja Sugawara-jinja.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{Center|Sugawara Shrine}}]] [[File:Isasumi-jinja Aizu-okunitama-jinja.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{Center|Aizu Okunitama Shrine}}]] * Sugawara Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|qid=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} ** Annual festival: {{ill|lt=February 25th|Q2356|qid=Q2356|1=February 25}} ** Monthly visit: 25th of every month *: Suesha {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|p=45}}. It is enshrined in the middle of Omotesando. * Aizu Okunitama Shrine ** Deity enshrined: God of the soul of Aizu Okuni ** Annual festival: {{ill|lt=May 15th|Q2562|qid=Q2562|1=May 15}} ** Monthly festival: 15th of every month *: Suesha . It is enshrined on the east approach outside the east shrine gate. Founded in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}58 ({{ill|lt=1983|Q2434|qid=Q2434|1=1983}}). The deities enshrined refer to the eight million gods who followed the development of Aizu by Obiko no Mikoto and Takenuma Kawabetsu no Mikoto. . * Sesshoseki Inari Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Uganomitama|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukatamakami}} ** Regular festival: First afternoon of February ** Monthly festival: 1st day of every month*: Suesha {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|p=45}}. It is located on the south side of the precincts, across the road. It is said that it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}12 ({{ill|lt=1841|Q7622|qid=Q7622|1=1841}}) to appease the curse caused by Sesshoseki. === Kyougaisha === "History of Isasumi Shrine" {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|p=45}} lists the following 12 shrines as subsidiary shrines outside the precincts. * Setsusha ** '''Sai Shrine'' - Takada Kamimachi. Regular festival: August 25th. *Suesha ** Suwa Shrine - Sabugawa. Regular festival: August 27th. ** Rokuyama Shrine - Sparrow Forest. Regular festival: September 1st. ** Godate Kumano Shrine - Sakaino. Regular festival: September 8th. ** Kumano Shrine - Yasuda. Regular festival: September 9th. ** Kasuga Shrine - Okinakata. Regular festival: September 9th. ** Inari Shrine - Akutsu. Regular festival: September 9th. ** Hie Shrine - Shinyashiki. Regular festival: September 9th. ** Inari Shrine - Terasaki. Regular festival: September 19th. ** Ikami Shrine - Sparrow forest. Regular festival: September 29th. ** Ota Shrine - Takada downtown. Regular festival: October 10th of the lunar calendar. ** Hachiman Hakusan Shrine - Saga Segawa. Regular festival: November 9th. === Festival ===
List of annual festivals
List of festivals held annually at Isasumi Shrine {{Sfn|History of Isasumi Shrine|1979|pp=43-45}}Book of History of the Shrine. . Major festivals and important rituals are shown in bold. * Every day ** Hikusai (Daily morning) * Monthly ** Moon Head Festival (1 day) **Monthly Festival (15th) * January ** Happo Festival (January 1st) **Saidan Festival (January 1st) - Middle Festival. **New Year Festival (January 1st to 7th) **Genshi Festival (January 3rd) - Middle Festival. **First Tenjin (January 25th) *February **Setsubun Festival (Setsubun Day in February) ** Sesshoseki Inari Shrine Annual Festival/Hatsuuma Festival (first afternoon of February) ** Tenjin Festival (February 25th) * March ** Plaque commemorative festival (March 15th) **Vernal Equinox Festival (Vernal Equinox in March) *April ** Fire Extinguishing Festival (April 15th) - Middle Festival ** Showa Festival (April 29th) - Middle Festival ** Prayer Festival (April 29th) - Grand Festival **'''Flower Festival''/Daida Kagura (April 29) -Dadai Kagura is an important intangible folk cultural property designated by Aizumisato Town. * May **'''Sunayama Festival''' (May 5th (Boys' Festival)) - Middle Festival** Aizu Okunitama Shrine Annual Festival (May 15th) *June *Iris Festival (June 15th to July 5th) *Summer Purification (June 30th) *July **'''Rice Planting Festival''' (July 11th to 13th) - Grand festival. Nationally important intangible folk cultural property. *August * Sai Shrine Annual Festival (August 24th to 25th) *September ** Sake Festival (September 1st) **Lantern Festival (September 2nd) **'''Reitaisai''' (September 1st to 17th) - Taisai. ** Autumn Equinox Festival (Autumn Equinox Day in September) * October ** Nukiho Festival (October 9th) **Kannaho Festival (October 17th) - Middle Festival. *November ** Meiji Festival (November 3rd) - Middle Festival. **Hakusan Shrine Annual Festival (November 15th) **'''Ninamesai'' (November 23rd) - Grand festival. **Ota Shrine Annual Festival (November 31st) *December ** Shutenjin (December 25th) **New Year's Eve Purification, New Year's Eve Festival, Old Shintatsu Burning Festival (December 31st)
; Sunayama Festival: ``Sunayama Festival''. It is held on {{ill|lt=May 5th|Q2550|qid=Q2550|1=May 5}} ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Boy's Festival|1=UNKNOWN}}). Since ancient times in the Aizu region, it has not been easy to find salt, so this is a Shinto ritual in which salt is made as a prayer to avoid being inconvenienced by salt. As a secret passed down from one company to another, the viewing is held in secret.{{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=528-529}}. During the festival, two sand mounds are built in the northeast corner of Tamagaki, and a {{ill|lt=shinto|Q60587|qid=Q60587|1=Himorogi}} is erected on the back sand mound to invite the Aiden deities, Shiogama Okami and Hachiman Okami, and three octagonal stakes and a hammer are placed on the front sand mound {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Vol. 7|1999|pp=528-529}}. Two priests perform the ritual in strange appearances wearing king nose masks and cockscombs {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=528-529}}. ; Rice planting festival: ``Otaue Festival''. To be held on {{ill|lt=July 12|Q2688|qid=Q2688|1=July 12}}. It is a festival ({{ill|lt=Otaue Festival|Q11489226|qid=Q11489226|1=Otaue Festival}}) where people pray for a good harvest by performing a simulated rice planting ceremony. Since ancient times, ``the morning rice planting of {{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, the afternoon rice planting of Takada, and the evening rice planting of {{ill|lt=Atsuta Shrine|Q482065|qid=Q482065|lt=Atsuta|1=Atsuta Shrine}} {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=529-531}}. There are two types of Otaue Festivals: the pre-celebration type, which is held during the New Year period, and the warm-country type, which is held during the rice-planting season.In the Tohoku region, where rice cultivation has a short history, many Ota-ue Festivals are pre-celebration type.{{Sfn|Otaue Festival (annual event)|2009}}, but perhaps because Aizu opened early, the warm-country type is held at Isasumi Shrine.{{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=652-653}}.: The festival is held after rice planting by general farmers {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine Otaue Festival (annual event)|2009}}. The festival begins with a Shinko Festival held at the main shrine, followed by young lion chasers going around the town and stirring the muddy fields of Ota Shrine to drive away the boars that are harmful to the rice crops. Afterwards, the mikoshi was carried from the head office to Ota Shrine with a procession of about 200 people. At Ota Shrine, a mikoshi is placed in front of the Goshosaku field, and in front of it {{ill|lt=Haimaraku|Q1428885|qid=Q1428885|1=Saibara}}, rice planting dance, lion dance, and Chigo dance are offered.{{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine Otaue Festival (annual event)|2009}}. The next day, early in the morning, the rice planting in the rice fields is repaired, and the festival ends with the Sanae Festival held at the head office {{Sfn|Isasumi Shrine Rice Planting Festival (annual event)|2009}} This festival, along with the rice planting ritual at Keitoku Inari Shrine} (Kitakata City), has been designated as a national important intangible folk cultural property as the ``Otaue Festival of Aizu.'' === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property (designated by the country) === * Vermilion lacquered gold-bronze mikoshi (craft) *: {{ill|lt=Mikoshi|Mikoshi|qid=Mikoshi|ltq=Q979940}} said to have been created during the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|qid=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}. It is said that it was dedicated in the year 6 ({{ill|lt=1526|Q6325|qid=Q6325|1=1526}}) by the father and son Moriyasu and Moritsune Ashina, the owners of the Takada mansion. The roof is clad in Hogai-zo copper plates, and the pillars are made of gilt bronze. The front door is painted with vermilion lacquer and has the gilt bronze emblems of ``Kenfutsubiki'' and ``Samitsu Tomoe'' on it, and on three sides other than the front, there are copper plates with the gilt bronze wheel treasure crest engraved on them, and a perimeter surrounds them. The parapets around the perimeter are made of gilt bronze, and there are stairs and gilt bronze torii gates on all sides. This mikoshi has the characteristics of the late Muromachi period, and is considered to be an excellent item from the same period. *: Currently stored and exhibited in the treasure hall of Isasumi Shrine. Designated on July 3, 1945 [http://www.town.aizumisato.fukushima.jp/11,5774,176.html List of nationally designated cultural properties] (Aizumisato Town homepage). {{Nationally designated cultural properties database|201|5173|Vermilion lacquer gold bronze mikoshi}}. === Important intangible folk cultural property (designated by the country) === * Aizu's Otaue Festival - Includes the rice planting ritual at Isasumi Shrine and the rice planting ritual at {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Keitoku Inari Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Kitakata City). Designated on March 28, 2019 {{Nationally designated cultural properties database|312|00000959|Aizu Otaue Festival}} === Fukushima prefecture designated cultural property === * Important cultural property (tangible cultural property) ** 1 pair of wooden guardian dogs (craft) **: A pair of {{ill|lt=guardian dogs|Q20155846|qid=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} estimated to have been created during the {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}. Each one is 97 centimeters in height, and is a rare giant wooden guardian dog in Japan. The statue is made of marquetry and painted with lacquer. It has a small head and a large body, and is different from those popular in the Heian and Kamakura periods {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=650-651}}. It is currently stored and displayed in the Treasure Hall. Designated on October 1, 1952 [http://www.town.aizumisato.fukushima.jp/11,5776,176.html List of prefectural designated cultural properties] (Aizumisato Town homepage). . * Natural monument ** Fuji at Isasumi Shrine - Designated on March 30, 1999{{PDFlink|[http://www.town.aizumisato.fukushima.jp/resources/content/7628/20091118-153441.pdf Fuji at Isasumi Shrine]}} (Aizumisato Town homepage). . === Cultural property designated by Aizumisato Town === * Important cultural property (tangible cultural property) ** 1 golden door (craft) **: A golden door that appears to have been made during the Muromachi period. The size is 161.8 cm in length, 55 cm in width, and 3.8 cm in thickness. The door is smaller but more luxurious than the ancient door, and it is presumed that it was originally the door of the main shrine. It is inconceivable that this door could have been made solely by the efforts of commoners and believers, and it is pointed out that there was a great deal of support from the feudal lord. In ancient documents, the shrine building was burnt down in {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|qid=Q1324885|1=Bunki}}3 ({{ill|lt=1503|Q6141|qid=Q6141|1=1503}}), and in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|qid=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}}11 ({{jalink|1514 It appears that the shrine was rebuilt by father and son {{ill|lt=Moriji Ashina|Q11625151|qid=Q11625151|1=Moriji Ashina}} and {{ill|lt=Morishige Ashina|Q11625149|qid=Q11625149|lt=Moriji|1=Moriji}} in the year |Q6204}}, and this door is presumed to have been created at that time. The shrine building was also burnt down in {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|qid=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1783|Q7745|qid=Q7745|1=1783}}), but it seems that the shrine survived the disaster {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=661-662}}. It is currently stored and displayed in the Treasure Hall. Designated on March 1, 1970 [http://www.town.aizumisato.fukushima.jp/11,5775,176.html List of Town Designated Cultural Properties] (Aizumisato Town Home Page). . ** 1 ancient door (craft)**: A door that appears to have been made during the Muromachi period. It is a gilt-bronze door made of red lacquer with a camellia pattern, and measures 173 centimeters in length, 49.5 centimeters in width, and 6 centimeters in thickness. It is presumed that the door was originally used in the inner sanctum of the main shrine. Like the Golden Door, this door was rebuilt in 1514, and is thought to have survived the fire in 1783. {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|p=662}} It is currently stored and displayed in the Treasure Hall. Designated on March 1, 1972 . ** 1 pair of Tekka tables (craft) **: Iron {{ill|lt=Kanamyo|Q1135870|qid=Q1135870|1=Huabiao}} (meaning {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|qid=Q234731|1=Torii}}). It is cylindrical and hollow inside, with a total length of 193 cm and a circumference of 196 cm. According to "Aizu old affairs miscellaneous notes", it is said to have been built by a man named Nagamine Ecchu on October 26, {{ill|lt=Meio|Q1193841|qid=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} first year ({{ill|lt=1492|Q6679|qid=Q6679|1=1492}}), and it has both pillars but no rainbow beam. It is also mentioned in "{{ill|lt=Shinpen Aizu Fudoki|Q11503941|qid=Q11503941|1=Shinpen Aizu Fudoki}}", and from these documents it is assumed that it is a tube used to insert the legs of a torii gate. On the other hand, there is also a legend that these are the pillars left after the lanterns were broken {{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=664-665}}. Designated on April 1, 1994 . ** Japanese Kisho Banquet Waka Volume 1 (ancient document)**: {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, {{ill|lt=waka|Q737148|qid=Q737148|1=Waka (poetry)}} in ink and paper. Total length 5.5 meters. The foreword is written by Ankyu Hattori, the waka poem is written by {{ill|lt=Korezari Yoshikawa|Q4053782|qid=Q4053782|1=Yoshikawa Koretari}}, and the afterword is written by Kiyoo Mizuno. On August 17, {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}10 ({{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|qid=Q7606|1=1670}}), the lord of Aizu, {{ill|lt=Masayuki Hoshina|Q837236|qid=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, became a vassal and a priest. This is a collection of waka poems that were composed about the contents of the lecture at a banquet (shokuen) held when Ankyū Hattori held a lecture on the ``{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihonki|1=Nihon Shoki}} Jindai Scrolls'' by Ankyū Hattori, who gathered 42 people, including 42 people, in the castle grounds. At this time, 42 people were reciting waka and one person was reciting {{ill|lt=Chinese poetry|Q1759242|qid=Q1759242|1=Shi (poetry)}}{{Sfn|Aizu Takada Town History Volume 7|1999|pp=666-667}}. Designated on March 1, 1972 . * Important intangible folk cultural property ** Taidai Kagura - Designated on March 25, 1975 . * Historic sites ** Isasumi Shrine Okumiya area - Designated on June 20, 1964 . * Natural monument** Usuzokuzakura - Designated on April 1, 1972 . Registered as a Fukushima Prefecture Green Cultural Property on September 13, 1981.{{PDFlink|[http://www.town.aizumisato.fukushima.jp/resources/content/7628/20091118-154026.pdf Usuzumi Sakura]}} (Aizumisato Town Home Page). . ** Isasumi Shrine Shrine - Designated April 1, 1994 .
